attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with sudden onset severe right-sided lower abdominal pain. She reports a positive home pregnancy test one week ago and estimates she is six weeks pregnant. She has a history of left-sided ectopic pregnancy two years ago requiring left salpingectomy. She has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable but haemodynamically stable. Her pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 115/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Her abdomen is tender in the right iliac fossa with guarding but no signs of peritonism. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus and a right adnexal mass with a gestational sac but no free fluid in the pelvis. Emergency laparoscopy confirms an unruptured ectopic pregnancy in the right fallopian tube.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate surgical management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with an ectopic pregnancy in her only remaining fallopian tube, having previously undergone left salpingectomy. The key consideration in management is preserving future fertility while safely treating the current ectopic pregnancy.

Right salpingostomy is the most appropriate management as it involves making a linear incision in the fallopian tube to remove the ectopic pregnancy while preserving the tube itself. This approach maintains the possibility of future natural conception, which is particularly important given this is the patient's only remaining fallopian tube.

Right salpingectomy would remove the only remaining fallopian tube, eliminating the possibility of natural conception in the future. While this would provide definitive treatment of the ectopic pregnancy, it would be overly aggressive in this scenario where the tube appears otherwise healthy and fertility preservation is likely desired.

Right partial salpingectomy involves removing a segment of the tube, which would still result in loss of tubal function and future fertility. This approach offers no advantage over complete salpingectomy in this case.

Systemic methotrexate would not be appropriate as the patient has already undergone laparoscopy, and direct surgical management would provide more immediate and definitive treatment with the opportunity to preserve the tube.

Right tubal ligation would be inappropriate as it would eliminate future fertility potential through the remaining tube.

Following salpingostomy, the patient will require close monitoring of beta-human chorionic gonadotropin levels to ensure complete resolution of the ectopic pregnancy. The risk of persistent trophoblastic tissue after salpingostomy is approximately 5-20%, and the risk of recurrent ectopic pregnancy in the same tube is around 15%.
learningPoints: Salpingostomy is preferred over salpingectomy in patients with only one remaining fallopian tube who wish to preserve fertility.
Close monitoring of beta-human chorionic gonadotropin levels is essential after salpingostomy to ensure complete resolution.
The risk of recurrent ectopic pregnancy in the same tube after salpingostomy is approximately 15%.
references: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy: Green-top guideline no. 21. BJOG. 2016 Nov;123(13):e15–55. Available from: https://doi.org/10.1111/1471-0528.14189
